root_cause: Notification delays were caused by an autoscaler regression introduced in version 2026.03.18.3 that averaged visible queue depth across all shards. After a large tenant migration concentrated traffic on shard 7, total backlog and message age rose sharply, but the autoscaler observed only a low per-shard average and did not add capacity until rollback restored total-backlog-based scaling.

details: Workers report only shard 7 aging out while provider probe logs stay healthy
Autoscaler decision logs explicitly show low observed depth despite growing queue age
notification_worker.total_queue_depth rises past 2200 while notification_autoscaler.observed_visible_depth remains near 120 until rollback
SES probe latency stays flat, reducing confidence in an external provider slowdown
Commit 1a7b210 changed the autoscaler to average visible depth across shards
Commit 912d4f2 moved a large tenant onto shard 7, creating the skewed load shape
Tenant migration precedes the incident context, autoscaler rollout precedes symptom acceleration, and rollback immediately changes scaling behavior
Only a subset of tenants saw delays, which fits a hot-shard scenario rather than a fleet-wide dependency failure

recommended_actions: Scale notification workers on total backlog plus per-shard oldest-message-age, not averaged shard depth alone
Add safeguards that detect skewed shard load after tenant migrations
Canary autoscaler logic against synthetic hot-shard traffic before production rollout
expected_investigation_path: Check whether delivery providers are degraded or whether delays are localized to a shard
Compare worker backlog metrics with the autoscaler's own observed signal
Review recent autoscaler commits and recent shard-mapping operational changes
Confirm causality from deployment timing and recovery after rollback-induced scale-up
